revision.text:
{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |Image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |ImageSize = 250 |ImageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} |PrincipalImage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens aqqeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April 11, 2011)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-- '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kozanji|ja|甲山寺}}

'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens a11eared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April 11, 2011)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺}}

{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} |PrincipalImage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>

{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} |PrincipalImage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺}}

'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺}}

It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).

== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.

revision.text:
{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} |PrincipalImage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|12=draft}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市|12=draft}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=draft}} |PrincipalImage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市|12=draft}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=draft}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=draft}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道|12=draft}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅|12=draft}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.

{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|12=draft}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市|12=draft}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=draft}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市|12=draft}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=draft}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|12=draft}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=draft}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道|12=draft}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅|12=draft}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|12=draft}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=draft}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 出釈迦寺 |画像 = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |画像サイズ=250 |画像説明 = 本堂・大師堂 |所在地 = [[香川県]][[善通寺市]]吉原町1091番地 |位置 = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=出釈迦寺}} |山号 = 我拝師山 |院号 = 求聞持院 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[真言宗御室派]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[釈迦如来]] |創建年 = |開山 = |開基 = （伝）[[空海]]（弘法大師） |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = 我拝師山 求聞持院 出釈迦寺 |別称 = |札所等 = [[四国八十八箇所]]73番 |文化財 = |公式HP = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ |公式HP名 = 我拝師山 求聞持院 出釈迦寺 |地図2={{Switcher | {{Location map+|Japan Kagawa|width=230|caption= |float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=13|lat_sec=9.8|lon_deg=133|lon_min=45|lon_sec=1|mark=Indian_Swastika.svg|marksize=12|position=left|label=出釈迦寺|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=21|lat_sec=2.46|lon_deg=134|lon_min=2|lon_sec=48.98|mark=blue pog.svg|position=top|label=[[高松駅 (香川県)|高松駅]]|label_size=95}} {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=12|lat_sec=51|lon_deg=134|lon_min=0|lon_sec=56|mark=BSicon FLUGg.svg|marksize=10|position=bottom|label=[[高松空港]]|label_size=95}}}} | 香川県における位置 | {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=230}} | 善通寺市内での位置 }}}} '''出釈迦寺'''（しゅっしゃかじ）は、[[香川県]][[善通寺市]]にある[[真言宗御室派]]の[[寺院]]。我拜師山（がはいしざん）、求聞持院（ぐもんじいん）と号す。[[本尊]]は[[釈迦如来]]。[[四国八十八箇所]]第七十三番[[札所]]。 * 本尊[[真言]]：のうまく さんまんだ ぼだなん ばく * [[ご詠歌]]：迷いぬる六道衆生（ろくどうしゅじょう）すくわんと 尊き山に出づる釈迦寺（でら） * 納経印：当寺本尊、奥之院捨身嶽跡、七ヶ所参り恵比寿神、奥之院粟嶋社、奥之院蛭子社、久延毘古神（山田のかかし） == 概要 == 弘法大師が真魚と呼ばれていた7歳の時に身を投じた山は奥の院捨身ヶ嶽禅定として、また、大師が虚空蔵菩薩求聞持法を修めたところとして、大師出家の原点であり一代記の中には必ず記される霊跡として大師信仰の重要な大願成就の場所となっている。 == 歴史 == 寺伝によれば、[[空海]]（弘法大師）が7歳の時に倭斬濃山（わしのやま）（現、我拝師山）に登り、「仏門に入って多く人と衆生を救いたいのです。私の願いが叶うなら釈迦如来様、お姿を現して下さい。もし、願いが叶わないのなら私の命を仏に捧げます」と願い、山の断崖から谷へと飛び降りた。すると、落下する空海の前に釈迦如来と天女が現れて抱きとめ、「一生成仏」と宣し、彼の願いが成就された。成人の後に空海は思い出の霊験の山に再度登って、釈迦如来が現われた山を「[[我拝師山]]」と名づけ、その山に出釈迦寺を建立し、釈迦如来の尊像を刻んで本尊としたという。
現在の当寺の奥之院である我拝師山山上のお寺は、かつては曼陀羅寺の奥之院<ref>澄禅の四国遍路日記に「曼陀羅寺ノ奥院ト可云山なり」とある</ref>であった。1653年巡拝の澄禅のとき麓に寺はなく、屏風を立てたる様の山上に登って少なき平らな所の昔の堂の跡に釈迦如来・石像文殊・弥勒の石像など有りと表現される所に参拝していて、ここに近年お堂を造立しようとしたが一夜にして吹き崩れ割れた板や瓦が多しという状態であった。その後、麓に宗善（～1690年寂）が寺を建立し<ref>1689年刊行の寂本の『四国遍礼霊場記』に「むかしより堂もなかりきを、ちかき比宗善という・・麓に寺を建立せりとなり」とある</ref>、山上が札所であるが麓の寺で納経するようになっていた<ref>1687年出版の真念の『四国遍路指南』に「此寺札打所十八町山上に有、しかれども由緒有て堂舎なし、ゆへに近年ふもとに・・寺をたつ、ここにて札をおさむ」とある</ref>。そして、[[1920年]]（[[大正]]9年）に札所は麓の寺に移された。
2023年、弘法大師生誕1250年を記念して、大正時代に札所と本尊を麓に移したとき造られたとみられる奥の院安置の秘仏・釈迦如来坐像、地蔵堂安置の宗善のとき造立の弘法大師像<ref>台座の墨書きに宗善他数十人の寄進者名があり</ref>が麓の本堂横で初公開され、あわせて奥の院安置されていた市文化財・不動明王立像も公開され、また、大師堂の弘法大師像の顔が目まで見えるまですだれを引き上げられた。
== 伽藍 == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|求聞持大師]] * 境内への参道：信者寺ゆえ、参道の左側には多くの寄進された石像や記念石や句碑（山頭火「山あれば山を観る」など4基）が並び季節の花が咲く。 * [[山門]]：2008年落慶。 * [[本堂]]：すだれ越しにうっすらと本尊釈迦如来坐像が浮かび上がる。脇陣に虚空蔵菩薩坐像（着色であったが修繕のとき金箔貼に）と不動明王立像（身体は青色で衣は赤に着色）。軒下のホウキは、釈迦の十代弟子の愚鈍な周利槃特(チューダ・パンタカ）は自分のことを愚かだと知っておりいつもホウキで掃除ばかりしていたが、優秀な兄の摩訶槃特(マハー・パンタカ)より先に悟りを得た解脱者になったという逸話から。
* [[大師堂]]：鼻から下の顔が拝顔できる。大師像のまとう緋色の衣は定期的に新調される。 * 地蔵堂：地蔵菩薩石像（坐像） * 鐘楼堂：2017年5月21日開眼 * 奥之院遥拝所（おくのいんようはいじょ） : 奥の院である捨身ヶ嶽禅定の遥拝所。石碑には、身を投げる空海とそれを救う釈迦如来と天女の図が描かれている。この場所で念仏を唱えると、捨身ヶ嶽禅定に登ったのと同じ利益（りやく）が得られると言われており、捨身ヶ嶽禅定の建物を望める。
山門を進み右に折れると左に納経所があり、正面に本堂、その右側が大師堂と並置されている。本堂の左の石段を上がると赤い壁の地蔵堂があり、その先に奥之院遥拝所と大きな石の台座に虚空蔵菩薩石像があり、眼前に奥の院のある我拝師山がそびえる。 * [[宿坊]]：なし * 駐車場：山門に上がる参道手前に広い駐車場が無料であり。 <gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|参道脇の石仏 Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|本堂軒下のほうき Syusyakaji02a.jpg|大師堂 Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|地蔵堂 Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|鐘楼堂 Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|奥之院遥拝所 Shusshaka-ji, Statue of Kobo-Taishi and Mt.ahaishi 001.jpg|弘法大師像と我拝師山 Shusshaka-ji, View from the temple grounds 001.jpg|境内からの眺め </gallery>
== 文化財 == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|禅定石造層塔]] ; 善通寺市指定有形文化財 * 木造不動明王立像 - 平成21年12月25日指定<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=善通寺市の指定文化財一覧 ／ 善通寺市 市内の指定文化財 |access-date=2023-06-29 |publisher=善通寺市役所 生涯学習課}}</ref>。 : 檜の寄木造り、彫眼、像高103cm、平安後期作。2009年10月16日に初めて公開されるまでは、絶対秘仏だった。 * 禅定石造層塔 - 平成23年4月11日指定<ref name=":0" />。 : 造立時期は8〜10世紀と推測
== 交通アクセス == ; 鉄道 * [[四国旅客鉄道]]（[[四国旅客鉄道|JR四国]]） [[土讃線]]{{Ndash}}[[善通寺駅]] (5.0 km) ; 道路 * 一般道：[[香川県道48号善通寺詫間線]] 吉原 (1.0 km) * 自動車道：[[高松自動車道]] [[善通寺インターチェンジ|善通寺IC]] (6.7 km) == 奥の院 == ; {{ウィキ座標|34|12|45.8|N|133|45|07.5|E|scale:10000|捨身ヶ嶽禅定|name=73 捨身ヶ嶽禅定}} (四国奥之院第一 禅定） : 我拝師山（標高481 m）と中山（ちゅうざん 標高439 m）の間に山門があり我拝師山側の標高380 mあたりにある。 : 出釈迦寺から約1 kmの登山口（駐車場あり）から世坂（よさか）を過ぎると柳の水があり、その上に3台ほどの駐車スペースを越え幾度も折り返しながら登ること徒歩約40分で奥の院「捨身ヶ嶽禅定」の山門に着く。その先の大きな建物が根本御堂（ねもとみどう）で昭和の初め頃、点在していた小さなお堂を統合して建てられた。この地は約300年前に麓に移されるまで札所であった。根本御堂の裏には小さな洞窟があり岩倉大師が祀られていて、さらに鎖場を越え岩場を約100 m登ると稚児大師石像があり、ここが最終目的地で幼き空海が身を投げたといわれる「捨身ヶ嶽のお行場」である。その途中には岩壁に彫られた目治篭彫不動尊をお見逃しなく。 : 根本御堂では、毎月旧暦の15日夜（7月のみ土用丑の日）に信者（誰でも参加可能）が集まり法要があり宿泊する。 : その毎月の法会の時は境内から奥の院まで、無料送迎車がでる。それ以外のときは、納経所に頼むと500円で奥の院山門直下駐車場まで自動車での乗り入れが許可される。（急坂の車道のため運転に自信がある人のみで、普通車以下） <gallery> Syusyakaji03.jpg|奥の院への参道 Gahaisisan06.jpg|奥の院と我拝師山頂上 Syusyakaji 20200904 01.jpg|根本御堂 Syusyakaziɨ0.jpg|目治篭彫不動尊 Syusyakaziɨø.jpg|稚児大師石像 Syusyakazi0œ.jpg|護摩壇 </gallery> * 世坂の句碑歌碑：西行「筆の山にかき登りても見つるかな 苔の下なる岩の気色を」他1歌が世坂駐車場の入口にあり、その上の壇に「与謝野晶子・寛庭園」があり4歌碑が、すぐ上の参道に5基と4基の燈籠に句碑や短歌が刻まれ、その上に澤田ふじ子「ゆく秋やほころびひどき頭陀袋」「わが許に大師ありますや遍路道」があり、更に上がると3基ある。そして上り切った本堂の前に大西きくゑの歌碑と句碑1基がある。
== 前後の札所 == ; [[四国八十八箇所]] : 72 [[曼荼羅寺 (善通寺市)|曼荼羅寺]] --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-- '''73 出釈迦寺''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 [[甲山寺]]

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称 = 出釈迦寺 |画像 = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |画像サイズ=250 |画像説明 = 本堂・大師堂 |所在地 = [[香川県]][[善通寺市]]吉原町1091番地 |位置 = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=出釈迦寺}} |山号 = 我拝師山 |院号 = 求聞持院 |宗旨 = |宗派 = [[真言宗御室派]] |寺格 = |本尊 = [[釈迦如来]] |創建年 = |開山 = |開基 = （伝）[[空海]]（弘法大師） |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = 我拝師山 求聞持院 出釈迦寺 |別称 = |札所等 = [[四国八十八箇所]]73番 |文化財 = |公式HP = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ |公式HP名 = 我拝師山 求聞持院 出釈迦寺 |地図2={{Switcher | {{Location map+|Japan Kagawa|width=230|caption= |float=center|places= {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=13|lat_sec=9.8|lon_deg=133|lon_min=45|lon_sec=1|mark=Indian_Swastika.svg|marksize=12|position=left|label=出釈迦寺|label_size=100|caption=}} {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=21|lat_sec=2.46|lon_deg=134|lon_min=2|lon_sec=48.98|mark=blue pog.svg|position=top|label=[[高松駅 (香川県)|高松駅]]|label_size=95}} {{Location map~|Japan Kagawa|lat_deg=34|lat_min=12|lat_sec=51|lon_deg=134|lon_min=0|lon_sec=56|mark=BSicon FLUGg.svg|marksize=10|position=bottom|label=[[高松空港]]|label_size=95}}}} | 香川県における位置 | {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=230}} | 善通寺市内での位置 }}}} '''出釈迦寺'''（しゅっしゃかじ）は、[[香川県]][[善通寺市]]にある[[真言宗御室派]]の[[寺院]]。我拜師山（がはいしざん）、求聞持院（ぐもんじいん）と号す。[[本尊]]は[[釈迦如来]]。[[四国八十八箇所]]第七十三番[[札所]]。 * 本尊[[真言]]：のうまく さんまんだ ぼだなん ばく * [[ご詠歌]]：迷いぬる六道衆生（ろくどうしゅじょう）すくわんと 尊き山に出づる釈迦寺（でら） * 納経印：当寺本尊、奥之院捨身嶽跡、七ヶ所参り恵比寿神、奥之院粟嶋社、奥之院蛭子社、久延毘古神（山田のかかし） == 概要 == 弘法大師が真魚と呼ばれていた7歳の時に身を投じた山は奥の院捨身ヶ嶽禅定として、また、大師が虚空蔵菩薩求聞持法を修めたところとして、大師出家の原点であり一代記の中には必ず記される霊跡として大師信仰の重要な大願成就の場所となっている。 == 歴史 == 寺伝によれば、[[空海]]（弘法大師）が7歳の時に倭斬濃山（わしのやま）（現、我拝師山）に登り、「仏門に入って多く人と衆生を救いたいのです。私の願いが叶うなら釈迦如来様、お姿を現して下さい。もし、願いが叶わないのなら私の命を仏に捧げます」と願い、山の断崖から谷へと飛び降りた。すると、落下する空海の前に釈迦如来と天女が現れて抱きとめ、「一生成仏」と宣し、彼の願いが成就された。成人の後に空海は思い出の霊験の山に再度登って、釈迦如来が現われた山を「[[我拝師山]]」と名づけ、その山に出釈迦寺を建立し、釈迦如来の尊像を刻んで本尊としたという。
現在の当寺の奥之院である我拝師山山上のお寺は、かつては曼陀羅寺の奥之院<ref>澄禅の四国遍路日記に「曼陀羅寺ノ奥院ト可云山なり」とある</ref>であった。1653年巡拝の澄禅のとき麓に寺はなく、屏風を立てたる様の山上に登って少なき平らな所の昔の堂の跡に釈迦如来・石像文殊・弥勒の石像など有りと表現される所に参拝していて、ここに近年お堂を造立しようとしたが一夜にして吹き崩れ割れた板や瓦が多しという状態であった。その後、麓に宗善（～1690年寂）が寺を建立し<ref>1689年刊行の寂本の『四国遍礼霊場記』に「むかしより堂もなかりきを、ちかき比宗善という・・麓に寺を建立せりとなり」とある</ref>、山上が札所であるが麓の寺で納経するようになっていた<ref>1687年出版の真念の『四国遍路指南』に「此寺札打所十八町山上に有、しかれども由緒有て堂舎なし、ゆへに近年ふもとに・・寺をたつ、ここにて札をおさむ」とある</ref>。そして、[[1920年]]（[[大正]]9年）に札所は麓の寺に移された。
2023年、弘法大師生誕1250年を記念して、大正時代に札所と本尊を麓に移したとき造られたとみられる奥の院安置の秘仏・釈迦如来坐像、地蔵堂安置の宗善のとき造立の弘法大師像<ref>台座の墨書きに宗善他数十人の寄進者名があり</ref>が麓の本堂横で初公開され、あわせて奥の院安置されていた市文化財・不動明王立像も公開され、また、大師堂の弘法大師像の顔が目まで見えるまですだれを引き上げられた。
== 伽藍 == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|求聞持大師]] * 境内への参道：信者寺ゆえ、参道の左側には多くの寄進された石像や記念石や句碑（山頭火「山あれば山を観る」など4基）が並び季節の花が咲く。 * [[山門]]：2008年落慶。 * [[本堂]]：すだれ越しにうっすらと本尊釈迦如来坐像が浮かび上がる。脇陣に虚空蔵菩薩坐像（着色であったが修繕のとき金箔貼に）と不動明王立像（身体は青色で衣は赤に着色）。軒下のホウキは、釈迦の十代弟子の愚鈍な周利槃特(チューダ・パンタカ）は自分のことを愚かだと知っておりいつもホウキで掃除ばかりしていたが、優秀な兄の摩訶槃特(マハー・パンタカ)より先に悟りを得た解脱者になったという逸話から。
* [[大師堂]]：鼻から下の顔が拝顔できる。大師像のまとう緋色の衣は定期的に新調される。 * 地蔵堂：地蔵菩薩石像（坐像） * 鐘楼堂：2017年5月21日開眼 * 奥之院遥拝所（おくのいんようはいじょ） : 奥の院である捨身ヶ嶽禅定の遥拝所。石碑には、身を投げる空海とそれを救う釈迦如来と天女の図が描かれている。この場所で念仏を唱えると、捨身ヶ嶽禅定に登ったのと同じ利益（りやく）が得られると言われており、捨身ヶ嶽禅定の建物を望める。
山門を進み右に折れると左に納経所があり、正面に本堂、その右側が大師堂と並置されている。本堂の左の石段を上がると赤い壁の地蔵堂があり、その先に奥之院遥拝所と大きな石の台座に虚空蔵菩薩石像があり、眼前に奥の院のある我拝師山がそびえる。 * [[宿坊]]：なし * 駐車場：山門に上がる参道手前に広い駐車場が無料であり。 <gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|参道脇の石仏 Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|本堂軒下のほうき Syusyakaji02a.jpg|大師堂 Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|地蔵堂 Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|鐘楼堂 Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|奥之院遥拝所 Shusshaka-ji, Statue of Kobo-Taishi and Mt.ahaishi 001.jpg|弘法大師像と我拝師山 Shusshaka-ji, View from the temple grounds 001.jpg|境内からの眺め </gallery>
== 文化財 == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|禅定石造層塔]] ; 善通寺市指定有形文化財 * 木造不動明王立像 - 平成21年12月25日指定<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=善通寺市の指定文化財一覧 ／ 善通寺市 市内の指定文化財 |access-date=2023-06-29 |publisher=善通寺市役所 生涯学習課}}</ref>。 : 檜の寄木造り、彫眼、像高103cm、平安後期作。2009年10月16日に初めて公開されるまでは、絶対秘仏だった。 * 禅定石造層塔 - 平成23年4月11日指定<ref name=":0" />。 : 造立時期は8〜10世紀と推測
== 交通アクセス == ; 鉄道 * [[四国旅客鉄道]]（[[四国旅客鉄道|JR四国]]） [[土讃線]]{{Ndash}}[[善通寺駅]] (5.0 km) ; 道路 * 一般道：[[香川県道48号善通寺詫間線]] 吉原 (1.0 km) * 自動車道：[[高松自動車道]] [[善通寺インターチェンジ|善通寺IC]] (6.7 km) == 奥の院 == ; {{ウィキ座標|34|12|45.8|N|133|45|07.5|E|scale:10000|捨身ヶ嶽禅定|name=73 捨身ヶ嶽禅定}} (四国奥之院第一 禅定） : 我拝師山（標高481 m）と中山（ちゅうざん 標高439 m）の間に山門があり我拝師山側の標高380 mあたりにある。 : 出釈迦寺から約1 kmの登山口（駐車場あり）から世坂（よさか）を過ぎると柳の水があり、その上に3台ほどの駐車スペースを越え幾度も折り返しながら登ること徒歩約40分で奥の院「捨身ヶ嶽禅定」の山門に着く。その先の大きな建物が根本御堂（ねもとみどう）で昭和の初め頃、点在していた小さなお堂を統合して建てられた。この地は約300年前に麓に移されるまで札所であった。根本御堂の裏には小さな洞窟があり岩倉大師が祀られていて、さらに鎖場を越え岩場を約100 m登ると稚児大師石像があり、ここが最終目的地で幼き空海が身を投げたといわれる「捨身ヶ嶽のお行場」である。その途中には岩壁に彫られた目治篭彫不動尊をお見逃しなく。 : 根本御堂では、毎月旧暦の15日夜（7月のみ土用丑の日）に信者（誰でも参加可能）が集まり法要があり宿泊する。 : その毎月の法会の時は境内から奥の院まで、無料送迎車がでる。それ以外のときは、納経所に頼むと500円で奥の院山門直下駐車場まで自動車での乗り入れが許可される。（急坂の車道のため運転に自信がある人のみで、普通車以下） <gallery> Syusyakaji03.jpg|奥の院への参道 Gahaisisan06.jpg|奥の院と我拝師山頂上 Syusyakaji 20200904 01.jpg|根本御堂 Syusyakaziɨ0.jpg|目治篭彫不動尊 Syusyakaziɨø.jpg|稚児大師石像 Syusyakazi0œ.jpg|護摩壇 </gallery> * 世坂の句碑歌碑：西行「筆の山にかき登りても見つるかな 苔の下なる岩の気色を」他1歌が世坂駐車場の入口にあり、その上の壇に「与謝野晶子・寛庭園」があり4歌碑が、すぐ上の参道に5基と4基の燈籠に句碑や短歌が刻まれ、その上に澤田ふじ子「ゆく秋やほころびひどき頭陀袋」「わが許に大師ありますや遍路道」があり、更に上がると3基ある。そして上り切った本堂の前に大西きくゑの歌碑と句碑1基がある。

revision.text:
{{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Zentsūji|ja|善通寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|ja|善通寺市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|ja|空海|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|ja|四国旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|ja|善通寺駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|ja|四国八十八箇所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|qq=|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|qq=|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>

In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.

<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>

== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
