The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).

This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>

In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11395826]] Wikidata name: Shusshaka-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji]] Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in [[:en:Zentsūji, Kagawa|Zentsūji, Kagawa]]. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]], as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * [[:en:Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|Main Hall]]: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|Shikoku Railway Company]] Dosan Line – [[:en:Zentsūji Station|Zentsūji Station]] (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]] : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * {{ill|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|en|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}}: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=Shikoku Railway Company}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|en|Zentsūji Station|WD=Q899921|lt=Zentsūji Station}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}

== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399}}}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * {{ill|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|en|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}}: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
== Cultural Assets == [[File:Syussyakaji 21.jpg|thumb|100px|Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda]] ; Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji * Wooden Fudō Myōō Standing Statue (designated December 25, 2009)<ref name="zentsuji">{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.zentsuji.kagawa.jp/soshiki/50/bunkazai.html |title=Designated Cultural Assets of Zentsūji |publisher=Zentsūji City Office}}</ref>. * Zenjō Stone Layered Pagoda (designated April pp, 20pp)<ref name="zentsuji" />.
== Access == ; By Train * {{ill|Shikoku Railway Company|en|Shikoku Railway Company|WD=Q496531|lt=Shikoku Railway Company}} Dosan Line – {{ill|Zentsūji Station|en|Zentsūji Station|WD=Q899921|lt=Zentsūji Station}} (5 km). ; By Road * Prefectural Route 48 (1 km from Yoshihara intersection). * Highways: 6.7 km from {{ill|Zentsūji Interchange|ja|善通寺インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zentsūji Interchange|1=Zentsuji interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10923476}} on the Takamatsu Expressway.
== Adjacent Temples == ; {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} : 72 {{ill|Mandaraji|ja|曼荼羅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mandaraji|qq=}} --{{small|(0.5 km)}}-* '''73 Shusshaka-ji''' --{{small|(2.2 km)}}-- 74 {{ill|Kōyama-ji|ja|甲山寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyama-ji|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.shusshakaji.com/ Official Website of Shusshaka-ji] * [https://www.88shikokuhenro.jp/73shusyakaji/ Shikoku Pilgrimage 73rd Temple]

== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * {{ill|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|en|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Shusshakaji]][[de:Shusshaka-ji]][[fr:Shusshaka-ji]][[ja:出釈迦寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Zentsuji|WD=Q191300|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.219388888889|N|133.75027777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}}} {{Japanese temple |Name = Shusshaka-ji |image = Syusyakaji20220316 1.jpg |imageSize = 250 |imageCaption = Main Hall and Daishi Hall |Location = {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}} Yoshihara Town 1091 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|13|9.8|N|133|45|1|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shusshaka-ji}} |Mountain = Gahaishi-san |TempleInstitute = Gomonji-in |Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} |Principalimage = {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}} |FoundedBy = (Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}) |TempleType = 73rd Temple of {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} |OfficialWebsite = https://www.shusshakaji.com/ }}
'''Shusshaka-ji''' (出釈迦寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in {{ill|Zentsūji, Kagawa|en|Zentsūji, Kagawa|WD=Q191300|lt=Zentsūji, Kagawa|1=Zentsūji, Kagawa}}. The temple's mountain name is Gahaishi-san (我拝師山), and its temple institute name is Gomonji-in (求聞持院). The principal image is {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha|qq=}}. It is the 73rd temple of the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|WD=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}}. * Principal mantra: "Nōmaku Sanmanda Bodanan Baku." * {{ill|Goeika|ja|ご詠歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goeika|1=eika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11267316}}: "Mayoi nuru rokudō shujō sukūanto / Totoki yama ni izuru Shakadera." * Stamps: Main image, Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Site, Seven Shrines Pilgrimage Ebisu Shrine, Oku-no-in Awashima Shrine, Oku-no-in Hiruko Shrine, Kuyebiko-no-kami (Yamada no Kakashi).
== Overview == Shusshaka-ji is said to be the site where {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}, as a child known as Mao, threw himself from a mountain at the age of seven. This mountain, now called Oku-no-in Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, is also where Kūkai is believed to have practiced the Kokūzō Bosatsu Gomonji method. It is considered the origin of his ordination and a sacred site recorded in his life’s history, making it an important place for Kūkai worship and prayer fulfillment.
== History == According to temple tradition, at the age of seven, Kūkai climbed Washinoyama (later named Gahaishi-san). He prayed, "If I am to enter the Buddhist path and save people and all living beings, please reveal your form, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha. If not, I will offer my life to you." He then leapt from a cliff. As he fell, Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha and celestial maidens appeared, caught him, and proclaimed his enlightenment. Later, Kūkai returned to this mountain, carved a statue of Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, and established the temple.
The current Oku-no-in at Gahaishi-san was historically the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji Temple<ref>Shozen’s pilgrimage diary refers to this as "the Oku-no-in of Mandaraji."</ref>. In 1653, when Shozen visited, there was no temple at the base of the mountain, and only remnants of ancient halls with statues of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, Monju, and Maitreya were visible on the summit. A monk named Sozen later built a temple at the base<ref>1689 publication "Shikoku Pilgrimage Sacred Sites Guide" records Sozen’s efforts.</ref>. By 1920, the pilgrimage site and main image were moved to the base.
In 2023, commemorating the 1250th anniversary of Kūkai's birth, the secret Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha statue from Oku-no-in and a statue of Kūkai from the Jizō Hall, attributed to Sozen, were publicly displayed for the first time.
== Grounds == [[File:Syusyakaziɨɨ.jpg|180px|thumb|Kūmonji Daishi]] * Entrance Path: The path is adorned with numerous stone statues and commemorative markers donated by followers. * [[Sanmon]]: Completed in 2008. * {{ill|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|en|Main_Hall_(Japanese_Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}: Features Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha faintly visible behind bamboo blinds, flanked by Kokūzō Bosatsu and Fudō Myōō statues. * {{ill|Daishi Hall|ja|大師堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi Hall|1=daishi-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11435234}}: Houses a Kūkai statue visible from the nose downward. * Jizō Hall: Includes a seated Jizō Bosatsu stone statue. * Bell Tower Hall: Consecrated on May 21, 2017. * Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site: A sacred spot to view Seshin-ga-take Zenjō, offering the same blessings as visiting Oku-no-in.
<gallery> Shusshaka-ji, Stone Buddha Statues 001.jpg|Stone Buddhas along the path Syussyakaji 20230406 1.jpg|Bamboo broom under the Main Hall Syusyakaji02a.jpg|Daishi Hall Shusshaka-ji, Jizo Hall 001.jpg|Jizō Hall Syusyakaji20220316 2.jpg|Bell Tower Hall Syusyakazi0ø.jpg|Oku-no-in Remote Viewing Site </gallery>
